questions.question: Which of the following best describes the String type in Java?
options: String is a primitive data type like int or double
String is a class in the java.lang package, and strings are objects
String is a special keyword that behaves like a primitive
String is an interface that must be implemented before use
questions.answer: String is a class in the java.lang package, and strings are objects

questions.question: What does it mean that Java strings are immutable?
options: Strings cannot be assigned to variables
Once a String object is created, its value cannot be changed
String variables cannot be reassigned to point to a different object
Strings can only be created using the new keyword
questions.answer: Once a String object is created, its value cannot be changed

questions.question: What is the String Pool in Java?
options: A collection of all String methods available in the API
A special area in memory where Java stores string literals to reuse identical strings
A thread pool dedicated to processing string operations
A design pattern for creating strings efficiently
questions.answer: A special area in memory where Java stores string literals to reuse identical strings

options: true, because both reference the same object in the String Pool
false, because == always returns false for strings
false, because each declaration creates a new object
questions.answer: true, because both reference the same object in the String Pool

options: true, because they contain the same characters
false, because new String() creates a separate object outside the String Pool
true, because Java interns all strings automatically
A compilation error occurs because you cannot compare strings with ==
questions.answer: false, because new String() creates a separate object outside the String Pool

questions.question: Which method should you use to compare the content of two strings?
What is the correct way to parse the String "42" into an int?
options: int x = Integer.valueOf("42").intValue(); // but parseInt is the standard way

questions.question: What happens when you call Integer.parseInt("hello")?
options: It returns the ASCII value of the first character

questions.question: Which of the following correctly converts an int to a String?
What is the difference between an empty string and a null string?
options: There is no difference; they are the same thing
An empty string is a valid String object with zero characters; null means the variable does not refer to any object
A null string has zero characters; an empty string has one space character
An empty string causes a NullPointerException when accessed
questions.answer: An empty string is a valid String object with zero characters; null means the variable does not refer to any object

questions.question: What happens when you call .length() on a null String variable?
Which of the following is a valid way to concatenate strings in Java?
options: Using the + operator or the .concat() method
questions.answer: Using the + operator or the .concat() method

options: A compilation error because you cannot add a string and an int

questions.question: What does Double.parseDouble("3.14") return?
What happens when you call Integer.parseInt("3.14")?
options: It returns 3 by truncating the decimal portion
A NumberFormatException is thrown because "3.14" is not a valid integer
questions.answer: A NumberFormatException is thrown because "3.14" is not a valid integer

options: a == d is true, a == f is true
a == d is false, a == f is false
a == d is true, a == f is false
a == d is false, a == f is true
questions.answer: a == d is true, a == f is false

questions.question: Why does "Ja" + "va" produce a string that is == to "Java", but a variable-based concatenation does not?
options: Because variable-based concatenation uses a different character encoding
Because concatenation of two literals is resolved at compile time and placed in the String Pool, while variable-based concatenation happens at runtime and creates a new object
Because the + operator works differently depending on the number of operands
Because the JVM always creates new objects for any concatenation
questions.answer: Because concatenation of two literals is resolved at compile time and placed in the String Pool, while variable-based concatenation happens at runtime and creates a new object

questions.question: What does Boolean.parseBoolean("true") return?
Which of the following statements about the == operator and strings is correct?
options: == compares the content of two strings
== compares the object references (memory addresses) of two strings
== always returns true if two strings have the same characters
== cannot be used with String variables
questions.answer: == compares the object references (memory addresses) of two strings

questions.question: How do you extract a char from the String "A" to store it in a char variable?

options: A compilation error because you cannot concatenate a String with an int

questions.question: Which method converts the int value 100 to the String "100"?
When a String method like toUpperCase() or replace() is called, what happens to the original string?
options: The original string is modified in place
The original string is unchanged; the method returns a new String object with the result
The original string is deleted from memory
The original string is moved to a different location in the String Pool
questions.answer: The original string is unchanged; the method returns a new String object with the result
